title: Introduction
description: NOC 64310 – Travel counsellors | GTR Immigration Travel counsellors under NOC 64310 are the professional navigators of Canada's tourism and corporate travel sectors. They provide critical expertise in itinerary planning, reservation management, and travel risk assessment. As the global travel landscape becomes more complex, the demand for personalized, human-led travel advice is resurging. For immigration, this TEER 4 occupation offers a specialized entry point into the Canadian labor market, with high eligibility for regional and provincial programs that support the hospitality and tourism industries.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category6 – Sales and service occupationsTEER Level4Major Group64 – Sales and service representatives and other customer and personal services occupationsSub-Major Group643 – Occupations in food, accommodation and tourismMinor Group6431 – Occupations in travel and accommodationNOC Code64310

duties: Provide travel information to clients regarding destinations, transportation, accommodation options, and travel costs.
Plan and organize vacation travel for individuals or groups.
Make transportation and accommodation reservations using computerized reservation and ticketing systems.
Investigate new travel destinations, hotels, and other facilities and attractions.
Provide travel tips regarding tourist attractions, foreign currency, customs, languages, and travel safety.
Sell single fare tickets and package tours to clients.
Promote particular destinations, tour packages, and other travel services.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
A college diploma or vocational training in travel or tourism is usually required.
Certification with the Association of Canadian Travel Agencies (ACTA) may be required by certain employers.
In Ontario, certification from the Travel Industry Council of Ontario (TICO) is required to provide travel advice to the public on behalf of a registered agency.
job_outlook: The outlook for travel counsellors is Stable. Over the 2022–2031 period, new job openings are expected to total approximately 8,700. While technology tempers some growth, retirements and the expansion of the niche luxury travel market ensure that experienced advisors will remain in demand.
labour_market_demand: The demand for travel counsellors is currently rated as Moderate to Good. While automated booking systems exist, there is a significant shift back toward professional counsellors for high-value vacation planning and corporate accounts. Demand is strongest in major urban hubs like Toronto, Montreal, and Vancouver, as well as in provinces with high tourism density such as Alberta and British Columbia.
Dynamic Industry: Work with global travel brands and advanced GDS (Global Distribution Systems).
Career Progression: Clear pathways into Travel Agency Management (NOC 60020) or specialized Corporate Travel Consulting.
Multicultural Client Base: Utilize multilingual skills to serve a diverse international community.
Travel Benefits: Access to specialized industry rates and "FAM" (familiarization) trips to global destinations.
pathways: Provincial Nominee Program (PNP): Entry-level and semi-skilled streams in provinces like BC and Saskatchewan.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A fast-track for counsellors with job offers in the Atlantic provinces.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Addressing tourism needs in participating small-town communities.
CEC (Canadian Experience Class): Accessible for those who have completed 12 months of work in Canada on a valid permit.
